title: Fine-Tuning Later Layers
concept: Fine-tuning later layers updates the more task-specific parts of a pretrained network so its higher-level features shift toward the new labels, domain, and decision boundary.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What do early layers in many pretrained vision networks often capture?

options.text: General patterns like edges and textures
Nothing useful until the last epoch

exercises.explain: Exactly. That is why later layers are often the ones we fine-tune for the new task.

cards.title: Tailor the suit, keep the fabric
cards.body: If a suit already has good material and structure, a tailor adjusts the fit instead of weaving a whole new fabric. Fine-tuning later layers works the same way: keep the broad base, then reshape the higher-level representation for the new task.

cards.title: What later-layer fine-tuning does
cards.body: Fine-tuning means continuing training on a pretrained model so some of its weights update for the new task. Engineers often fine-tune the later layers first because those layers tend to hold more task-specific combinations and concepts than the earliest feature detectors.

cards.title: Update the last chapters of the guidebook
cards.body: A travel guide may already explain airports, trains, and maps well. If you move to a new city, you do not rewrite the whole book. You mostly revise the chapters about local neighborhoods, restaurants, and routes. Later network layers are like those later chapters.

cards.title: From general animal photos to dog breeds
cards.body: A pretrained image model may already separate fur, faces, paws, and body shapes. But dog-breed classification needs finer distinctions than general animal recognition. Fine-tuning later layers helps the model shift from broad “animal” concepts toward the details that separate huskies, beagles, and poodles.

cards.title: Fine-tuning later layers is not starting over
cards.body: A common myth is that once you unfreeze later layers, the network forgets everything and relearns from zero. In reality, fine-tuning usually begins from a strong pretrained state. You are adjusting an existing representation, not erasing it and rebuilding from scratch.

cards.title: General base vs. specialized top
cards.body: Transfer learning often relies on a split between broad perception and task-specific interpretation.

left.text:
General edges and textures stay useful
Less risk of disturbing basic perception
Acts like a reusable foundation

right.text:
Higher-level features shift toward new labels
Adapts concepts to the new domain
Refines the model’s final understanding

exercises.prompt: Which layers are usually the best first candidates for task-specific adaptation in transfer learning?

options.text: Later layers, because they are often closer to the original task-specific decisions
None of the layers can ever be updated after pretraining
The layer names in the config file

exercises.explain: Correct. Later layers are often where the model’s representation becomes most tied to the original task.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Fine-tuning later layers means the model has lost all benefit from pretraining.
exercises.explain: False. Fine-tuning works precisely because it starts from a useful pretrained representation.

exercises.sentence: Compared with early layers, later layers are often more task-___.
exercises.explain: Yes. Later layers often carry more task-specific concepts, which is why they are a common place to adapt.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best shows fine-tuning later layers?

options.text: Unfreezing the upper blocks of a pretrained model so it can adapt from generic animal features to fine-grained bird species
Keeping every single weight fixed forever while changing nothing else
Replacing the dataset with random noise so the model learns faster

exercises.explain: Right. The key idea is adjusting the higher-level representation for the new task.

exercises.prompt: A pretrained model already recognizes broad plant patterns, but your new task must separate very similar leaf diseases. What transfer-learning move best fits that need?

options.text: Fine-tune the later layers so the higher-level features can adapt to the subtle disease differences
Randomize all weights so the model forgets the old visual knowledge
Freeze the entire network and refuse any adaptation
Remove the final classifier and stop there without training

exercises.explain: Exactly. When the new task needs more specialized distinctions, later-layer fine-tuning is often the right lever.
